readingMaterial.text: Every great journey starts with a single word. In Python, the print() function is how your program "speaks" to the world.
readingMaterial.html: Every great journey starts with a single word. In Python, the print() function is how your program "speaks" to the world.

readingMaterial.text: 1. How it Works
To make Python say something, you put your message inside the parentheses () and wrap text in quotes ("").
readingMaterial.html: To make Python say something, you put your message inside the parentheses () and wrap text in quotes ("").

readingMaterial.code:
# This makes the computer say Hello!
  print("Hello, Voyager!")

readingMaterial.text: 2. Why use Quotes?
Think of quotes as a "container" for text (which programmers call a String). Without them, Python thinks you are looking for a secret variable name instead of just reading the words.
readingMaterial.html: Think of quotes as a "container" for text (which programmers call a String). Without them, Python thinks you are looking for a secret variable name instead of just reading the words.

items: Correct: print("Magic") -> Output: Magic
Incorrect: print(Magic) -> Error! (The computer is looking for a variable named Magic)

readingMaterial.text: 3. Printing Numbers
Numbers don't need quotes! Python treats them as mathematical values automatically.
readingMaterial.html: Numbers don't need quotes! Python treats them as mathematical values automatically.

questions.question:
What will happen if you run the following code? 
print(Hello)
options: It causes an Error because there are no quotes.

readingMaterial.text: The Quest: To awaken the system, you must command the computer to speak. Your task is to use the print() function to display a classic greeting for all coders.
readingMaterial.html: The Quest: To awaken the system, you must command the computer to speak. Your task is to use the print() function to display a classic greeting for all coders.

readingMaterial.text: Instructions:
readingMaterial.html: Instructions:

items: Use the print() function.
Inside the parentheses, type the phrase "Hello World" (don't forget the double quotes!).

readingMaterial.text: Expected Output
readingMaterial.html: Expected Output

readingMaterial.text: Hello World
readingMaterial.html: Hello World

questions.question:
The Quest: To awaken the system, you must command the computer to speak. Your task is to use the print() function to display a classic greeting for all coders.
Instructions:
Use the print() function.
Inside the parentheses, type the phrase "Hello World" (don't forget the double quotes!).
questions.preCode: # Type your code below to print the word Hello World

readingMaterial.text: In Python, a Variable is like a labeled jar. You can put a value (like a word or a number) inside the jar and give it a name so you can find it later.
readingMaterial.html: In Python, a Variable is like a labeled jar. You can put a value (like a word or a number) inside the jar and give it a name so you can find it later.

readingMaterial.text: 1. How to Create a Variable
You use the = sign. This isn't math; it’s an assignment. It means "Take the thing on the right and put it in the jar on the left."
readingMaterial.html: You use the = sign. This isn't math; it’s an assignment. It means "Take the thing on the right and put it in the jar on the left."

readingMaterial.code:
# Create a jar named 'hero' and put "Link" inside
hero = "Link"

readingMaterial.text: 2. Using the Jar
Once you’ve stored a value, you can use the name of the jar instead of the value itself!
readingMaterial.html: Once you’ve stored a value, you can use the name of the jar instead of the value itself!

readingMaterial.text: 3. The Golden Rules of Naming

items: Names must be one word: No spaces! (Use player_score, not player score).
No quotes for names: Only the value inside gets quotes if it's text.
player = "Zelda" (Correct)
"player" = "Zelda" (Error!)

questions.question:
Look at the following code:

power = 9000
print(power)

readingMaterial.text: The Quest: You have found a powerful artifact! To keep it in your inventory, you must assign its name to a variable.
readingMaterial.html: The Quest: You have found a powerful artifact! To keep it in your inventory, you must assign its name to a variable.

readingMaterial.text: Instructions:
readingMaterial.html: Instructions:

items: Create a variable named artifact.
Assign the text "Golden Compass" to it (Remember the quotes!).
Use the print() function to display your artifact variable.

questions.question: t
questions.preCode:
# Step 1:  Assign "Golden Compass" to the variable artifact
 # Step 2: Print the artifact variable
# Don't use quotes inside the print()! Use the variable name.
artifact = ?
print(?)

readingMaterial.text: Not all data is created equal. Imagine trying to multiply the word "Apple" by the number 5—it doesn't make sense! Python uses Data Types to keep things organized.
readingMaterial.html: Not all data is created equal. Imagine trying to multiply the word "Apple" by the number 5—it doesn't make sense! Python uses Data Types to keep things organized.

readingMaterial.text: 1. The Scroll (String)
Any text wrapped in quotes (" ") is a String.
readingMaterial.html: Any text wrapped in quotes (" ") is a String.

items: Example: "Level 1", "Dragon", "100" (Yes, even a number in quotes is treated like text!)
Rule: You can't do math with Strings.

readingMaterial.text: 2. The Stone (Integer)
A whole number without quotes is an Integer.
readingMaterial.html: A whole number without quotes is an Integer.

items: Example: 5, 100, -20
Rule: You can perform math (like addition or subtraction) on these.

readingMaterial.text: 3. Why it Matters
Look what happens when we use the + sign:
readingMaterial.html: Look what happens when we use the + sign:

items: String + String: "10" + "10" becomes "1010" (It just glues them together!)
Integer + Integer: 10 + 10 becomes 20 (Actual math!)

questions.question: Which of the following is an Integer?

readingMaterial.text: The Quest: A fellow alchemist left you a message, but they used the wrong "container"! They stored a player's age as a String (text), but to enter the Forbidden Library, the age must be an Integer (number) so the computer can calculate if you're old enough.
readingMaterial.html: The Quest: A fellow alchemist left you a message, but they used the wrong "container"! They stored a player's age as a String (text), but to enter the Forbidden Library, the age must be an Integer (number) so the computer can calculate if you're old enough.

readingMaterial.text: Instructions:
readingMaterial.html: Instructions:

items: Look at the variable age_text—it is currently a String because of the quotes.
Create a new variable called age_number.
Assign the value 15 to age_number, but make sure it is an Integer (no quotes!).

questions.question: t
questions.preCode:
# This is a String (Text)
age_text = "15"

# Step 1: Create age_number and assign it the value 15 as an Integer
age_number = ?
  
 # Don't change the line below
print(f"Access Granted! Age verified as: {age_number}")
questions.solution:
age_number = 15
print(f"Access Granted! Age verified as: {age_number}")

readingMaterial.text: In Python, you can perform calculations just like you would on a calculator. These symbols are called Operators.
readingMaterial.html: In Python, you can perform calculations just like you would on a calculator. These symbols are called Operators.

readingMaterial.text: 1. The Basic Four

items: Addition (+): 10 + 5 becomes 15
Subtraction (-): 20 - 5 becomes 15
Multiplication (*): 3 * 5 becomes 15 (We use the asterisk, not an 'x'!)
Division (/): 30 / 2 becomes 15.0

readingMaterial.text: 2. Using Variables in Math
The real power comes when you use Variables instead of just raw numbers. Python will look inside the "jar," grab the value, and do the math for you.
readingMaterial.html: The real power comes when you use Variables instead of just raw numbers. Python will look inside the "jar," grab the value, and do the math for you.

readingMaterial.code:
apples = 5
price = 2

# This will calculate 5 * 2
total_cost = apples * price

print(total_cost) # Output: 10

readingMaterial.text: 3. Order of Operations
Python follows standard math rules (PEMDAS). It does multiplication and division before addition and subtraction unless you use parentheses ().
readingMaterial.html: Python follows standard math rules (PEMDAS). It does multiplication and division before addition and subtraction unless you use parentheses ().

questions.question:
Look at the following code:
score = 10
bonus = 5
final_score = score + bonus * 2

What is the value of final_score?

readingMaterial.text: The Quest: Your character has taken some damage in a duel! You need to calculate your Final Health by taking your starting health and subtracting the damage received.
readingMaterial.html: The Quest: Your character has taken some damage in a duel! You need to calculate your Final Health by taking your starting health and subtracting the damage received.

readingMaterial.text: Instructions:
readingMaterial.html: Instructions:

items: You are given a variable current_health set to 100.
You are given a variable damage set to 30.
Create a new variable called final_health.
Set final_health to be the result of current_health minus damage.

questions.question: t
questions.preCode:
current_health = 100
damage = 30

# Step 1: Calculate the final health
final_health = ?
  
# Don't change the line below
print(f"Final Health: {final_health}")
questions.solution:
current_health = 100
damage = 30
final_health = current_health - damage
print(f"Final Health: {final_health}")
